story.start:
Welcome to Tales of Visteria.

A story DM'd by Jubikino
The world of Visteria was created by MolarCapybara
An adaptation made by Redxjak

Content warning: vulgar language and dark fantasy themes.

Choose New Game to begin, or Load Game to continue a saved journey.
story.dm_intro:
You look down from above at the puny mortals whose fate you ultimately control. You see them in a caravan and decide they need some excitement.

What trouble do you send?
story.dm_send_hydra:
You send the hydra.

Haha! Did you see the way their eyeballs popped from their skulls? 
Then the smell of their burning flesh, ah that was great... 

I should make some popcorn.
story.dm_send_orcs:
Wow, they ran like little bitches, but they survived. Fine, I guess I get to keep playing with their lives.

Do I let them go to the caves of darkness and despair, or the forest of life, gifts, and power?
story.dm_choose_forest:
Let's be nice and send them to the Forest of Gifts, power, and healing...

Ah, I crack myself up. Cave of despair they go!
story.dm_choose_caves: Caves was always the option. The caves are really the whole story.
story.dm_ghost:
Alright, what are these fuck tards going to do now? Let's give them this creepy-ass ring girl and see what they do.

Who do I want to watch?
story.dm_watch_cletus_low: Cletus does not think. He just rushes to the ghost girl, tries to swing his axe, and falls on his ass. Hahahaha, I cannot believe he did that.
story.dm_watch_cletus_high: Cletus is actually good with kids and convinced her to go "play" with the orcs coming in. Oh boy, I did not expect that
story.dm_watch_cal_low: Cal tried to sneak past her and tripped on his robe. Not that it mattered. She knew you were there, dude. Guess you are going to have to fight.
story.dm_watch_cal_high: Way to take the initiative, Cal! That magic missile to the chest is a quick way to take out a ghost.
story.dm_watch_ren_low: Ren, you moron. You have the best eyesight and can see it is a ghost, yet you decided to throw a fucking stick at it? Dude, you are so dead.
story.dm_watch_ren_high: Well, you talked to her like a dad and convinced her to help, so I guess that works. I am not sure how helpful it was for her to tell you about some "pointy sticks."
story.dm_watch_kili_low: Oh, dwarf guy. Kili, I think. I kind of forgot about you. Let's see what you d... dude, no fucking way. Did you really try to kiss the ghost? That is not your goth baddie!
story.dm_watch_kili_high: You are the only one who could have recognized that she was a 200-year-old princess and promised to save her kingdom. Nice work, dude.
story.dm_watch_continue: Alright, they got past the creepy dead kid. I guess I need to see where these idiots wander next.
story.dm_districts:
They make it to the old road sign.

Left goes to the barracks. Right goes to the merchant district.

Okay, this is where I get to be a little mean. Do I send them toward pointy sticks and angry orcs, or toward a market full of goblins who have absolutely never paid taxes in their lives?
story.dm_barracks:
The barracks are shaped like a colosseum because of course the dwarves built a training ground like they were compensating for something.

Three orcs patrol the center ring. There is an armory, living quarters, and a very obvious chance for someone to do something stupid.

What do I make interesting?
story.dm_barracks_armory_low: They trip over a shield. A shield. The thing made to stop people from getting hit is now the reason they are about to get hit. Beautiful.
story.dm_barracks_armory_high: They actually sneak into the armory. Fine. Let them feel competent for twelve seconds.
story.dm_barracks_quarters_low: They open the first door and find a goblin in a dress. He screams. I did not plan that, but I am absolutely keeping it.
story.dm_barracks_quarters_high: Empty room. Kind of boring, honestly, but not everything can be a haunted child or a murder chest.
story.dm_barracks_combat:
Three orcs notice them immediately.

I would say good luck, but I control the luck, so that feels dishonest.

Combat starts: three orcs.

Assuming they win, I will let them loot the bodies because I am generous and handsome and definitely not making that up.
story.dm_barracks_loot_low: Nothing. Perfect. They dug through a bunch of old military junk and found disappointment.
story.dm_barracks_loot_mid: A health potion. Practical, boring, and exactly the kind of thing they will forget to use until it is too late.
story.dm_barracks_loot_high: They find a map. Oh wow, look at them, solving my death bridge before they even get there.
story.dm_barracks_map:
They find a brittle old military route map. It only has the bridge sequence intact: {directions}.

I made a whole trapped bridge and the nerds found instructions. Fantastic. I hope they remember it before the rest of the parchment gives up on existing.
story.dm_merchant:
They go right into the merchant district.

The market is wrecked, the stalls are dead, and five goblins with two orcs are wandering around like this is a field trip for ugly little criminals.

How do I want this to go?
story.dm_merchant_shop_low: The shop door squeaks so loud it might as well yell, "Hey monsters, free adventurers inside." Excellent door work.
story.dm_merchant_shop_high: The door opens quietly. The owner kept it well-oiled, which means this dead city has better maintenance than most living towns.
story.dm_merchant_sneak_low: A war horn sounds. They have been spotted. That is what happens when you try to sneak through a street like you are in a parade.
story.dm_merchant_sneak_high: They actually sneak past. No one sees them. I am proud and annoyed.
story.dm_merchant_combat:
Five goblins and two orcs notice them.

This is a lot of monsters, but they chose violence, and I respect bad decisions when they are funny.

Combat starts: five goblins and two orcs.

If they survive, I will let them loot the bodies.
story.dm_merchant_loot_low: They find gold, rotten fruit, and dwarven ale. That ale is either treasure or a war crime. Probably both.
story.dm_merchant_loot_mid: They find a health potion. Look at that, responsible adventuring. Disgusting.
story.dm_merchant_loot_high: They find a map. There goes my bridge trap mystery. I swear these people only roll high when it bothers me.
story.dm_merchant_map:
They find a brittle old military route map. It only has the bridge sequence intact: {directions}.

The rest is torn away, because apparently even my handouts have commitment issues.

They should remember it or write it down before the parchment gives up on existing.
story.dm_residential:
They reach the residential district.

There are houses ahead, which means doors, and doors mean players will absolutely open the worst one available.

I have options.

Large Manor - crashing, yelling, and probably loot.
Small Shack - quiet, suspicious, and not worth the smell.
Large House - clean, perfect, full of chests, and definitely fine. So fine. Super normal.
Skip all that and go to the bridge.

What do I want to watch?
story.dm_manor:
The manor is loud inside. Something is crashing around, which is either monsters fighting or furniture having a nervous breakdown.

There is a black chest in there too, because nothing says "please open me" like a terrible idea with hinges.

Do I want to give them a real prize or a lesson?
story.dm_manor_orb:
They unlock the chest and find a pure magistone orb pulsing with power.

That thing is absolutely going to matter later. Or explode. Maybe both. I have not decided, and that is the joy of being God with snacks.
story.dm_manor_boom:
The black chest detonates.

That is what happens when you trust a mystery box in a dead city. Honestly, I feel like the chest was very clear about its whole vibe.
story.dm_shack:
They open the small shack and find a gremlin and a ghoul doing some kind of... ritual.

Nope. Not explaining that. Combat starts.

Fight setup: one gremlin and one ghoul.

If they win, there is nothing worth taking, unless someone wants emotional damage in a bag.
story.dm_mimic_house:
They open the big clean house.

It feels safe. It feels welcoming. It has chests sitting right there.

This is bait so obvious it should have a sign, which means someone will still bite it.
story.dm_mimic_burn:
They set the house on fire.

Honestly, fair. If a building feels too friendly in my campaign, burning it down is a valid form of insight.
story.dm_mimic_loot:
They open a chest and it has teeth.

Yes. There it is. That beautiful moment when treasure becomes dental work.

Fight setup: one mimic first. If they survive, the other two mimics come alive and attack.

If they survive the whole stupid house, I will drop a torn map piece with the first two bridge directions and a pulsing magistone orb.
story.dm_mimic_rest:
They sleep in the mimic house.

I almost feel bad. Almost.

The house turns into a mouth and starts eating them. This is what happens when you nap inside suspicious architecture.
story.dm_bridge:
They finally reach the bridge to the King's Castle.

It is massive, hot as hell, and built over a lava lake because apparently the dwarves wanted OSHA to have a stroke.

Broken wagons, dead monsters, old traps, magistone lanterns, orc patrols. Gorgeous. Terrible. Very me.

The safe route through the trap section is {directions}. If they forget that after I basically handed them a map, I am allowed to laugh.

Now we get to see if they can cross without announcing themselves to every ugly thing on the bridge.
story.dm_bridge_sneak_start:
They step onto the bridge and try to move through the smoke.

Goblins mutter near one of the lanterns. An orc turns around at the worst possible time, because that is what orcs are for.

Let us see if these heroes remember how shadows work.
story.dm_bridge_sneak_low: They get spotted almost immediately. Incredible. Just full volume adventuring across the murder bridge.
story.dm_bridge_sneak_high: They slip between patrols like they actually read the rogue chapter of the rulebook. Good for them. Annoying for me.
story.dm_bridge_patrol:
A goblin spots movement and shrieks. The nearest patrol turns toward them with weapons raised.

Fight setup: one orc and two goblins.
story.dm_warehouse:
About a quarter of the way across, they find a warehouse built into the bridge.

Inside, a ritual is already happening. Silver mask in the middle, bronze masks around him, three cultists guarding the room.

Oh good. A cult meeting. Those are always normal and never involve a horrible thing clawing its way into reality.

Fight setup: three cultists.
story.dm_warehouse_leader_rage:
The last guard drops. The ritual sputters.

The silver-masked leader turns toward their sack like he can smell the haunted doll through leather.

"You! You have it. Give it to me!"

Dude, relax. Or do not. Actually, do not. Rage villains are easier to aim at.

Fight setup: silver-masked ritual leader.
story.dm_silver_mask_choice:
The leader collapses, and the silver mask hits the ash.

There it is. Shiny, cursed-looking, cold, and practically screaming, "I am a future consequence."

What do these brave geniuses do with the obviously dangerous face object?
story.dm_silver_mask_left:
They leave the mask in the ash.

Respectable. Suspiciously responsible, even.
story.dm_silver_mask_stored:
They wrap the mask and store it away.

It feels colder than it should, which is fantasy language for "you will regret this later, but in an interesting way."
story.dm_silver_mask_worn:
They put the mask on.

Oh hell yes. Face-first into cursed mystery. That is the kind of poor decision-making that keeps a campaign alive.

The bridge gets quiet around them, and power moves through their bones like cold lightning.
story.dm_ritual_surge:
The ritual circle pulses.

Once.

Twice.

Blood markings crawl across the floor. The air vibrates. A hole tears open in the middle of the circle, and darkness starts leaking out like the world has sprung a very bad pipe.

Alright, heroes. Panic creatively.
story.dm_warehouse_leave:
They run for the exit while the warehouse shakes itself apart.

Then the thing starts coming through.

One huge ashen head. Then another. Then another. Too many necks, teeth, and pale throats dragging themselves into the world.

False Hydra. Because apparently regular memory horror was exactly enough today.

And then...

Everything stops.
story.dm_false_hydra_interruption:
The monster freezes. The ash freezes. Even the lava stops moving.

That is when the Order shows up.

Twelve armored figures. Twelve perfect white masks. Walking in like they own the scene, which is rude, because I was using that scene.

The leader looks at the false hydra, then at the party, and basically says, "You found something nasty."

No shit, white-mask guy. I put it there.
story.dm_order_dialogue:
The Order does that thing powerful NPCs do where they answer just enough to be annoying.

This manifestation belongs to them now. The ritual belongs to them now. The warehouse belongs to them now.

Excuse me? I am the DM. You cannot just walk into my bridge warehouse and confiscate my horror monster.

Then POP.

Warehouse gone. Ritual gone. False hydra gone. Just empty bridge and a lot of unanswered questions.

Fine. Very mysterious. I hate how cool that was.
story.dm_warehouse_stop_ritual:
They attack the ritual circle and break one of the living blood lines.

The hole screams without a mouth, which is always a fantastic sign.

For one beautiful second, it looks like the tear might collapse.

Then it gets bigger.

Faster.

Hungrier.

The chanting stops, but the ritual keeps going anyway, which is rude and also very plot-relevant.

Good job, weirdos. You turned the emergency into a countdown.
story.dm_warehouse_jump_hole:
They jump into the hole.

I am sorry, they what?

No. Absolutely not. That is not bravery. That is putting your whole body into the plot blender.
story.dm_bridge_end:
They make it to the far stretch of the bridge.

Somehow, they survived the lava bridge, patrols, warehouse cult, silver mask, ritual hole, possible false hydra, and twelve dramatic white-mask weirdos.

I would say I am proud, but then they might get confident, and confidence is how players start licking magic items.

Current checkpoint options:
* Rest after the bridge sequence
* Put the silver mask back on if they stored it
* Move on to the orc camps
story.dm_silver_mask_reworn:
They put the silver mask back on.

The cold settles over their face again. Relief first. Then power.

Oh, that is definitely healthy. No concerns. Ten out of ten cursed accessory.
story.dm_bridge_end_rest:
They find a sheltered spot between two dim magistone lanterns and breathe for a minute.

The heat does not fade. The silence behind them is still wrong.

But fine. They can have a rest. I am merciful sometimes, mostly when it makes the next bad thing funnier.
story.dm_orc_camps_preview:
They reach the orc camp. Tents on one side, barrels on the other, three orcs around a fire in the middle. Classic stealth-or-violence buffet.

Good news: the next horror buffet is prepped now. Bad news: I am the chef.
story.dm_castle_approach:
They creep close enough to peek through the broken front gate.

The courtyard is packed with orcs, and three ritualists move among them like officers. Masks in firelight. Quiet commands. Immediate obedience.

If the silver mask is available, it starts doing the cursed item equivalent of marketing copy.
story.dm_castle_put_on_mask: Oh no. They put the mask on and the orcs called them master. That is definitely not going to feed anyone's worst instincts.
story.dm_castle_main_gate_mask:
The ritualists greet them as someone above them. The orcs lower their weapons and ask for orders.

The mask whispers about owning all of this, which is a normal healthy thing for face metal to say.
story.dm_castle_main_gate_fight:
They walk through the main gate without the mask.

Three ritualists. A courtyard full of orcs. Every head turns at once.

Combat setup: deeply unfair. Inspirationally stupid.
story.dm_castle_side_entrance:
They find the side entrance, which is the first intelligent routing decision anyone has made in a while.

Rest, checkpoint, ruined kitchen, clean cloth, water, storage with 30 gold and two health potions. I am generous when it makes the later horror hit harder.
story.dm_servant_quarters:
Servant quarters. Empty rooms, dead dwarves, one ugly little ambush, and then the lore starts tapping the glass.

A torn portrait shows the king beside a young princess holding the same doll. A brittle note says the princess would not sleep without her little doll and kept saying it wanted to see the king.
story.dm_doll_reveal: The doll is in the sack even if they left it behind, because haunted objects respect consent about as much as players respect obvious danger signs.
story.dm_throne_room:
The crown is still on the king's skull. Nobody touched it. That is either excellent discipline or the biggest warning label in history.

Bones on the throne. Black darkness blasted into the floor. No footprints. No orc scratches. Nobody lingers here because everything in the castle knows better.
story.dm_crown_temptation:
The mask starts whispering about crowns and kingship.

Wonderful. The cursed authority mask has discovered upward mobility.
story.dm_false_hydra_final:
The doll drops into the black bloom. The stitched mouth opens. A child's voice says, "Daddy?"

And there it is. The anti-story with red eyes. Final boss time.

story.caravan:
Your caravan crawls through the dusk road to Visteria.
Then horns split the air. Goblins rush from the brush. Orcs break the line.
You fight beside the wagons until the guards are scattered and the road is lost.

Do you stand and fight, or run for the ravine?
story.caravan_fight: You plant your feet beside the burning wagons as an orc and two goblins push through the smoke.
story.caravan_combat_victory: The orc drops last, leaving the road briefly clear around you.
story.caravan_run: You dive through the burning canvas and sprint toward the ravine.
story.caravan_overrun: The caravan is overrun. Escape is the only thing left.
story.escape:
You are still being chased.
Ahead stands a faded signpost, its words scraped away by time.

One path leads into a forest with gray miasma. The other drops into a cave.
story.escape_forest:
The forest breathes a gray miasma.
Your thoughts loosen. Branches bend into familiar shapes.
After hours, you stagger back to the same blood-stained road, still being chased, as if no time passed.
story.escape_forest_repeat: The forest will not let you pass. The cave waits like a mouth.
story.escape_cave: You slip into the cave as shouts rise behind you.
story.cave:
The cave descends into an ancient buried city. Everything here is dead.
Behind you, goblin voices echo closer.

Do you fight them, or go deeper?
story.cave_fight_start: Two goblins scramble into the cave behind you, blades drawn and eyes wide in the dark.
story.cave_fight_success: You hold the narrow passage and drive them back.
story.cave_fight_fail: They overwhelm your guard before fleeing from something farther below.
story.cave_deeper: You hurry deeper until the voices fade.
story.ghost_girl:
Little girl with white face and black hair standing in the corridor.

What do you do?
story.persuade_mid:
She giggles, points to the merchant district and says "There's a fun glowy ball that way."

When you look back, she is gone and a doll is in her place.
story.persuade_high:
You tell her you are being chased and ask for her help. She disappears and drops a doll. You hear terrified screams of orcs and goblins.

You carefully, but swiftly, walk deeper, unwilling to turn back to the horrors you can only imagine going on to the monsters behind you.
story.ghost_black_pits_death: Her eyes turn to black empty pits of despair. You feel as if you are staring into a black hole that sucks you in. Nothing matters to you. Soon you lose your sense of self. You lose your life.
story.ghost_black_miasma: Black miasma like smoke pours from her and she disappears into it. Before long that disappears as well.
story.ghost_play: She looks at you and giggles "Play?"
story.fight_girl_warrior:
You let out a mighty war cry, raise your axe and run at the apparition, with the knowledge that nothing can stop you.

You aimed for the head and swung through it like an axe through air, and keep going. You pass completely through her and fall to the ground. Yup you were right, nothing could stop you.
story.fight_girl_scholar:
You feel for the swirling core of potential in your soul and feel the power there. With an extension of your will, you manifest it in a blast of power and hurl it at the unknown specter in front of you.

She lets out an ear-piercing scream that disorients you. You feel blood coming out of your ears and have the worst headache of your life.
story.fight_girl_ranger:
You nock an arrow and pull back on the bowstring. You aim carefully at the little girl with the precision of a hunter and release the string.

Your arrow passes through her as if nothing was there. Your keen eyes notice a slight distortion where the arrow flew through.
story.fight_girl_dwarf:
Damn, is that my goth baddie? I'm gonna give her a kiss.

You run as fast as your short legs can take you to your 17.50
story.sneak_past_girl_start: You try to slip past her without drawing attention.
story.sneak_past_girl_result: She turns her head and watches you, even through walls. Once you get to a point past her, she instantly appears in front of you.
story.doll_choice: You hear the monsters getting closer. Do you grab the doll, or just run?
story.doll_grab: You quickly grab the doll and start running.
story.doll_leave: You turn away from the doll and run. You feel a shiver and sneak a quick glance back. The doll is gone.
story.search_city_low: You find a few gold coins, some rotten fruit, and a bottle of dwarven ale.
story.search_empty: Everything has been picked clean, there is nothing here.
story.throne_map:
You find a brittle old military route map. It marks the safe bridge path toward the King's Castle: {directions}. The rest of the route is torn away.

Remember it or write it down if you need to. The map is too brittle to keep checking later.
story.combat_potion: You drink a health potion and regain {healing} health.
story.combat_run_success: You break away from the fight and keep moving.
story.combat_run_consequence: The enemies do not wait politely for a rematch. They scatter, regroup, or drive you off course, and the chance to turn this exact fight into easy experience is gone.
story.combat_run_fail: You try to run, but the monsters cut off your escape.
story.combat_dodge: You focus on defense and try to avoid the next blow.
story.combat_attack_roll: Attack {attack_number}: d20 roll {natural} + {bonus} = {total} to hit {enemy} AC {ac}.
story.combat_hit: Damage roll: {damage_roll} = {damage}. You hit {enemy} for {damage_dealt} damage.
story.enemy_hit: {enemy} rolls {natural}, total {total} against your AC {ac}. Damage roll: {damage_roll} = {damage}.
story.enemy_miss: {enemy} rolls {natural}, total {total} against your AC {ac}, and misses.
story.combat_loot_map: Loot bodies to find a map.
story.level_up:
Level up!
+4 Health
Choose your level up reward.
story.level_up_body:
+4 Health
Choose your level up reward.
story.level_up_body_mana:
+4 Health, +4 Mana
Choose your level up reward.
story.level_hp: Your maximum health increases by another 4, and you feel tougher.
story.level_mana: Your maximum mana increases by another 4, and power returns to you.
story.level_attribute_unavailable: Only custom characters can increase attributes.
story.level_ac: You tighten your guard. Your armor class increases.
story.level_damage: Your strikes land harder. Your damage increases by 2.
story.level_heal: You catch your breath and recover to full health and mana.
xp.reason.doll_choice: made a choice about the doll
story.districts:
You get to the end of the road.
The sign points left to Barracks and right to the Merchant district.

Which route do you take?
story.districts_rest:
You find a sheltered corner near the old road sign and take a moment to breathe.

Your health is restored.
story.city_enter: You turn right toward the city district.
story.city_girl_hint: There's a fun glowy ball that way. The girl's whisper follows you between the old market stalls.
story.city_description:
You enter the city gates and find yourself in a market area, with some buildings that look like homes farther down the road.

A band of five goblins and two orcs roams nearby.
story.city_shop: You try to stay to the shadows and sneak into one of the open shops.
story.city_shop_fail: You reach for the door handle and push it open. A loud squeak echoes out into the city.
story.city_shop_success: You reach for the door handle and slowly push it open. It looks like the owner kept it well-oiled. Nice.
story.city_assault: You are immediately seen. Prepare for a fight.
story.city_sneak: You try to sneak past the monsters.
story.city_sneak_fail: You hear a war horn sound. You have been spotted.
story.city_sneak_success: You successfully avoid their gaze and sneak past them.
story.city_combat_victory: The last monster falls in the ruined market.
story.barracks_enter: You turn left toward the barracks.
story.barracks_girl_hint: Pointy sticks. The girl's whisper lingers in your head.
story.barracks_description: You make your way into the barracks. It is shaped like a colosseum. The outer ring holds an armory and living quarters, with a fighting pit in the center. Three orcs patrol the inner ring.
story.barracks_armory: You try to sneak to the armory.
story.barracks_armory_fail: You trip over a leftover shield. Prepare to fight.
story.barracks_armory_success: You successfully make it to the armory.
story.barracks_fight: The orcs immediately notice you and prepare to fight. Good luck.
story.barracks_quarters: You try to sneak to the living quarters.
story.barracks_quarters_fail: When you open the door to the first living area, you find a goblin in a dress. He screams and alerts the orcs.
story.barracks_quarters_success: You open the door and find it empty. You search around for anything useful.
story.barracks_combat_victory: The last orc falls in the fighting pit.
story.residential_enter: You continue on to the residential district.
story.residential:
You see a row of houses and hear noises coming from some of them, but you cannot tell how many enemies are inside.

You have a few choices:

Large Manor - You hear crashing and yelling.
Small Shack - It seems quiet, but doubtful to have anything of value.
Large House - It is in perfect condition. You do not hear anything, and through the windows you can only see a few chests. You feel a slight sense of power coming from the house.
Ignore the houses and go straight to the bridge.
story.residential_rest:
You find a defensible corner near the road to the bridge and take a final breather.

Your health is restored.
story.large_manor: You open the door and find two orcs directing a squad of five goblins to ransack the place and look for something.
story.large_manor_unseen: They have not seen you yet. What do you do?
story.large_manor_grouped: They are all crowded together, whispering about something. This is the perfect chance to take them all out.
story.manor_sneak: They have not seen you yet. What do you want to do next?
story.manor_group_kill_warrior:
You notice that none of them are paying attention. You scoff and think, "Guess my drill sergeant was right. Complacency kills."

With large, barreling steps, you run over to the group, who finally look up at you. But it is too late. You swing your massive axe through all of their bodies, bisecting tops from bottoms.
story.manor_group_kill_scholar:
Academically, you know that it is a sign of intelligence to focus on what you are working on, but you cannot help thinking the group in front of you are complete morons for ignoring their surroundings in a place like this.

Oh well. Not your problem. They look up in horror as you quick-cast a fireball that melts the skin from their bodies and kills them before they have the chance to raise arms against you.
story.manor_group_kill_ranger:
You are used to your prey not noticing you as you move silently. This is no different. You scan the room and see an angle where you can take them out quickly. If luck is with you, and it usually is, you should be able to kill them before they realize they have become the hunted.

You ready five arrows, nock the first one, slowly take aim, draw, and release. Draw and release. Draw and release. Draw and release. Draw and release. Five dead in the span of a breath.
story.manor_group_kill_dwarf:
"Well, who am I to ignore a gift from Moradin?"

You saunter over to the group and, without hesitation, knock the two orcs' heads together hard enough to daze a golem, kick a goblin in the stomach, grab another goblin by the throat, and use him to beat the other one to death.

You curb-stomp the first goblin and finish him off, then ensure the orcs will not be able to come after you later.
story.manor_group_kill_dm: The monsters are so focused on their search that you simply decide they are done being useful. They collapse before they understand the scene has changed.
story.manor_combat_victory: The last monster falls in the ruined manor.
story.manor_win: Bloodied and exhausted, you stand victorious. Now to see if this place held anything worth killing for.
story.manor_loot: You ransack the house and find a set of unmarked keys, a small black chest, and a newspaper detailing that the king has enacted a draft to support the war effort.
story.manor_rest:
With the manor finally quiet, you brace the doors and take a short rest among the wreckage.

Your health is restored.
story.manor_store_chest: You store the unmarked keys and the black chest for later. Whatever is inside can wait.
story.manor_chest_keys: The keys are unmarked. One of them must fit the chest.
story.manor_chest_trap: The chest was trapped and exploded with the force of a dragon's anus after a night of dwarven ale and a Crunch Roll Peasant Bell.
story.manor_chest_unlock: You unlocked the chest and notice that it was trapped. You do not want to imagine what would have happened if you had put the wrong key in.
story.manor_magistone_orb: You open the chest to find a pure magistone orb that seems to be pulsing with power.
story.small_shack: You open the door to find a gremlin and a ghoul connected in some kind of... uh, ritual. They notice you and attack.
story.small_shack_win:
The gremlin and ghoul fall, leaving the shack quiet and deeply uncomfortable.

There is nothing worth carrying out of here.
story.mimic_house:
You open the door to find a large room with tables, chairs, and a bar. Everything is exceptionally clean. Looking around, you see three chests spread out around the room.

A welcoming feeling invades your body. You feel that this is a safe place.
story.mimic_house_burn:
Being the little pyro that you are, you cast the low-level fire spell you learned as a child and set the house ablaze.

You walk away from a potentially disastrous situation and head to the bridge.
story.mimic_house_leave: You ignore the welcoming feeling, shut the door, and start walking away.
story.mimic_house_loot:
You go to the first chest and gleefully open it, only to see a chest full of shiny white teeth.

It is a mimic!
story.mimic_house_loot_clear: Your head suddenly clears, the welcoming sense fades, and you initiate combat with the mimic.
story.mimic_house_rest:
You set out your mat and lie down to rest. You are a lot more tired than you thought, and soon you fall fast asleep.

You wake up to three mimics eating your limbs, but you feel no pain. You look around and see that the inside of the house has morphed into the inside of a mouth. Acid coats the floor and slowly eats you away.
story.mimic_house_fight_one_win: The first mimic collapses with a wet, splintering groan.
story.mimic_house_second_wave: The other two mimics come alive and attack.
story.mimic_house_fight_two_win: The last mimic collapses in on itself, its splintered body twisting tighter and tighter until it forms a pulsing magistone orb. A torn shred of map slips free from the wreckage.
story.mimic_house_escape:
You take the magistone orb and the torn bridge map, then stumble out of the mimic house, bruised, slimed, and somehow alive.

The map reveals the first two directions to follow on the trapped bridge: {first}, then {second}.
story.mimic_house_escape_full_map:
You take the magistone orb and stumble out of the mimic house, bruised, slimed, and somehow alive.

A torn scrap of map slips free from the wreckage too, but it shows less than the brittle military route map you already found. You leave the useless scrap behind.
story.mimic_house_tongue_catch:
The doorway flexes behind you.

Before you can take a full breath, a thick tongue snaps out from the house and wraps around your waist. The whole building shudders, its windows squeezing shut like eyes as it starts dragging you back inside.

You have one chance before it pulls you into the mouth.
story.mimic_house_tongue_cut_success:
You hack into the tongue with everything you have. The blade bites deep, and the mimic-house shrieks hard enough to rattle the street.

The tongue releases you, twitching across the stones while you scramble away.
story.mimic_house_tongue_cut_fail:
You swing for the tongue, but the flesh is slick and stronger than it looks. Your blade skids sideways.

The house yanks you back through the doorway, and the door slams shut.
story.mimic_house_tongue_burn_success:
You force fire into your palm and press it against the tongue. The wet flesh sizzles, curls, and snaps away from you.

The mimic-house screams as you stagger free.
story.mimic_house_tongue_burn_fail:
You spark a flame, but the tongue folds over your hand before the spell can catch. The fire dies against slime and pressure.

The house drags you back inside, swallowing your scream with the closing door.
story.mimic_house_flee:
You turn and run to the front door, but it is locked. The inside of the house starts to slowly morph. The walls take on a pink hue and green liquid starts to leak. The floor has taken on a squishy appearance and feel. The back of the house, where the back door was, starts to elongate until all you can see is a black hole. You smell something awful and look down; the green substance has started eating away at your boots.

The house is a mimic!
story.mimic_house_black_hole: You choose the black hole. This is exactly as bad an idea as it sounds.
story.mimic_house_fire:
The flame catches the wet inner wall. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_fire_torch:
You rip a torch from your pack and jam it into the wet inner wall. The flame catches, stubborn and ugly. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_fire_flare:
You crack a flare and shove the hissing red light against the wet inner wall. Smoke bursts through the room. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_fire_cantrip:
You snap out a quick fire cantrip. The spark digs into the wet inner wall and blooms hot enough to hurt. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_fire_lantern:
You smash your lantern against the wet inner wall and kick the flame into the spilled oil. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_fire_spark:
You scrape together enough flame to catch the wet inner wall. The mimic screeches and spits you out into the street.

You stand up, disoriented but alive.
story.mimic_house_walls:
The walls are hard as steel, and the mimic seems unaffected.

The house squeezes tighter.
story.mimic_house_ale:
The 3000-proof dwarven ale is too much for the mimic to handle. It instantly gags and vomits you onto the street.

You turn back as it stands on long, skinny legs and wobbles away.
story.bridge:
You stand before a massive bridge leading toward the King's Castle.

It stretches across a lake of molten lava, its stone ribs disappearing into ash and smoke. Magistone lanterns line the sides, casting a dim blue glow over broken wagons, shattered barricades, and old dwarven defenses.

Orcs and goblins patrol the span in loose groups. Crossing will mean moving between shadows, hiding behind debris, and staying quiet whenever possible.
story.bridge_has_map:
The old map showed a safe route through the bridge traps, but the parchment has crumbled from being handled too much.
One final readable line remains: {directions}.
You will need to follow the directions from memory.
story.bridge_partial_map: The torn map reveals only the first two safe directions: {first}, then {second}. After that, you will have to trust your memory and nerve.
story.bridge_no_map: Without a map or memorized directions, it may be impossible to safely cross.
story.bridge_temp_end:
The far side of the bridge waits in smoke and drumbeats.

You have survived the warehouse ritual, the silver mask, the Order, and the impossible silence where the false hydra vanished.

Ahead, the orc camps choke the castle road.
story.bridge_end_rest:
You find a sheltered stretch of bridge between two dim magistone lanterns and finally let yourself breathe.

The heat never fades, and the silence behind you feels wrong, but for now you are alive.

Your health is restored.
story.warehouse_approach:
The safe path through the trapped bridge ends near a warehouse built into the stone ribs of the span.

Smoke presses against the doors. Blue magistone light leaks through cracks in the frame. Somewhere inside, voices chant in a low, wet rhythm.

You have a breath before you cross that threshold.
story.warehouse_rest:
You crouch behind a broken wagon near the warehouse wall and force yourself to slow down.

The heat of the bridge bakes through the stone, and the chanting inside does not stop, but you bind what you can, drink what water you dare, and steady your hands.

Your health is restored.
story.orc_camps_preview:
You leave the empty stretch of bridge behind and continue toward the far side.

Behind you, the bridge is wrong now. Too quiet. Too clean. The buried city's outer roads are sealed by rubble, old gates, and whatever the Order did to the warehouse.

You should be thinking about escape.

Instead, your eyes keep drifting toward the King's Castle.

A strange sense of duty settles over you, small at first, then heavier with every step. Someone still needs something from that place. You cannot explain how you know it.

Beyond the smoke, drums begin to sound from the orc camps.
story.orc_camps_approach:
The camps sprawl across the broken road like a wound that learned to build tents.

Smoke crawls low between hide shelters, stacked barrels, cookfires, barricades, and watch posts nailed together from dwarven beams. Orcs move in patrols of two and three, but they are not moving like raiders anymore.

They are guarding something.

A bronze-masked ritualist walks past a cookfire without looking at the orcs around it. One of them lowers his head immediately.
story.orc_camp_outer_choice:
A smaller outer camp blocks the path. A few tents stand to the left. Barrels are stacked in uneven rows to the right. In the center, five orcs sit around a fire, laughing through mouthfuls of meat.

You need to get past them.
story.orc_tents_success:
You slip between the tents, keeping low as smoke rolls over the camp. The orcs at the fire never look your way.

Near one tent flap, you hear a guttural voice mutter, "Masked ones said no one enters the throne wing."

Another answers, "Priests say the king wakes soon."

You keep moving.
story.orc_tents_fail:
You edge around a tent and your boot catches a shovel.

The handle clatters against a cooking pot.

A single orc bursts from the tent, blinking, angry, and already reaching for his axe.
story.orc_tent_orc_defeated: The orc falls before he can shout for the others. You drag him into the shadow of the tent and move on.
story.orc_barrels_success:
You press yourself behind the ale barrels and wait while two orcs stomp past.

One of them snorts and says, "Silver face gives orders. We follow."

The other grunts like that explains everything.

When they turn away, you slip past the stacked barrels and deeper into camp.
story.orc_barrels_fail:
You crouch behind the barrels and hold your breath.

For one perfect second, it works.

Then an orc grabs the barrel in front of your face and lifts it away.

Both of you freeze.

The orc bares his tusks and reaches for his weapon.
story.orc_barrel_orc_defeated: The orc drops beside the barrel he should have left alone. You step over him and hurry onward.
story.orc_fire_fight_start:
You walk straight toward the center fire.

Three orcs around it stop laughing one by one.

Then the first one stands, and the rest follow.
story.orc_fire_first_wave_defeated:
The third orc crashes into the firepit, kicking sparks into the smoke.

Before you can breathe, one more orc tears out of a nearby tent, weapon raised.
story.orc_fire_second_wave_defeated: The last orc falls. The camp around you is louder now, but the smoke and drums hide more than they reveal. You push deeper before the rest notice the bodies.
story.orc_camp_inner_path:
Past the outer camp, the path narrows between barricades of broken shields and old dwarven beams. The castle rises ahead, but one more stretch of camp lies between you and the gates.

Beyond the barricade, a much larger group of orcs drinks, shouts, and beats weapons against tables in a rough party area.
story.orc_party_area:
The party area is packed. Nearly thirty orcs crowd around tables, barrels, fires, and stolen dwarven banners. Some are drunk. Some are armed. Most are both.

A direct fight would be madness, but the noise might cover a careful path around them.
story.orc_party_sneak:
You keep to the edge of the party, moving with the smoke whenever the fire shifts. The orcs are too loud, too drunk, and too busy trying to impress each other to watch the shadows.

Near the back of the camp, you find a row of cages.
story.orc_party_distraction_choice:
You scan the party for something useful. A barrel of strong dwarven ale sits too close to a cookfire. Loose stones cover the ground near your feet.

You could try to make noise, or you could make a much bigger problem.
story.orc_party_ale_success:
The ale barrel catches, swells, and bursts with a heavy wooden boom. Burning drink sprays across the dirt.

Orcs leap up shouting. Half the party runs toward the fire. The other half laughs because they are idiots.

It is enough. You move past them in the confusion.
story.orc_party_ale_hurt:
The ale barrel catches too fast. The blast throws heat and splinters back into you.

You take {damage} damage.
story.orc_party_ale_fail:
The fuse gutters. The barrel smokes, pops once, and leaks ale into the dirt.

One orc nearby sniffs the air, shrugs, and goes back to drinking. The distraction does not work.
story.orc_party_rock_fail:
You throw a rock into the dark.

It clicks against stone somewhere beyond the fire.

No one hears it over the drums, shouting, drinking, and one orc loudly trying to sing through a mouthful of meat.

That did nothing.
story.orc_party_fight_start:
You step into the party area with your weapon ready.

The nearest orc laughs. Two more shove away from the table and come at you, eager to be first.
story.orc_party_vanguard_win: The three orcs hit the dirt hard enough to rattle cups across the nearest table.
story.orc_party_fight_warning:
For one bright, stupid second, it looks like you might have made a point.

Then the rest of the party notices.

Thirty orcs turn in your direction. The noise dies. Even by adventurer standards, continuing this fight would be aggressively educational.
story.orc_party_fight_disaster:
You tighten your grip and step forward anyway.

Fair enough. Some lessons require visual aids.
story.orc_party_win:
Somehow, impossibly, the last orc falls. The party area is no longer a party. It is a field of bodies, broken cups, and dying fires.

You move on before anything worse comes to investigate.
story.orc_cages:
Behind the party area, you find three cages.

Two hold dead bodies curled against the bars. The third holds someone alive.

The prisoner looks up at you with wide eyes and presses a finger to his lips.

The cage doors have hinges, bars, and crude latches, but no locks.
story.wazetax_rescue:
You ease the cage open and whisper, "Follow me. Quietly."

The prisoner nods so hard you worry his head might come loose. He crawls out, keeps low, and follows you past the party area.
story.wazetax_left:
You leave the prisoner in the cage. His eyes follow you until smoke hides him from sight.

You move on alone.
story.castle_front_wazetax:
You reach the front of the castle with the prisoner still crouched behind you. The broken gate looms ahead. Firelight moves across the walls like something breathing.

For the first time since the cages, he dares to whisper.
story.wazetax_who:
"My name is Wazetax," he whispers. "I am a tinkerer. I look for broken things, stuck doors, cracked locks, busted gearwork, bad hinges, old traps, anything like that. If I find a problem, I report it. If nobody is around to fix it, I try to help."

He swallows hard and glances back toward the camp.

"I am much better with tools than I am with orcs."
story.wazetax_happened:
"I do not know," Wazetax says. "I was wandering too close to the ruins. These orcs grabbed me, dragged me here, and threw me in that cage."

His voice breaks.

"I thought they were going to eat me."
story.wazetax_knows:
Wazetax shakes his head quickly.

"No. I have no idea what happened here. I do not even know what is going on. I just want to leave."
story.wazetax_hide:
"Okay," you whisper. "I have a feeling I have to go into the castle. I am going to try to get us out of here. Can you find somewhere to hide for now?"

Wazetax nods. "Okay. I can do that."

He slips around the side of the castle, where the wall falls into darkness.

"I am going to hide back here," he whispers. "Please come get me when you are ready."
story.castle_exterior_endpoint:
You reach the front of the King's Castle alone.

The broken gate looms ahead, black against the smoke. Firelight crawls across the walls like something breathing.

The strange sense of duty pulls at you again, not hard enough to force your feet forward, but hard enough to make leaving feel like betrayal.

For now, you stop before the castle and look up at it.

Chapter complete for now.
story.castle_exterior_endpoint_wazetax:
You and Wazetax reach the front of the King's Castle.

The broken gate looms ahead, black against the smoke. Firelight crawls across the walls like something breathing.

Wazetax says nothing for once. He only stares up at the castle, one hand pressed over his mouth, the other clenched around whatever courage he has left.

The strange sense of duty pulls at you again, not hard enough to force your feet forward, but hard enough to make leaving feel like betrayal.

For now, you both stop before the castle and look up at it.

Chapter complete for now.
story.castle_approach:
You creep close enough to peer through the broken front gate.

Beyond it, the castle courtyard crawls with orcs. Three ritualists move among them like officers, their masks catching the firelight as they issue quiet commands.

The orcs obey immediately.
story.castle_approach_mask_whisper:
The silver mask whispers from near you, cold and intimate.

"They are yours to command, if you wish it. Take your rightful place."
story.castle_put_on_mask:
The cold weight settles over your face.

The courtyard beyond seems quieter now.
story.castle_entrance_choice:
You circle the outer wall through weeds, rubble, and old scorch marks.

The main gate remains the obvious way in, which in Visteria means it is probably trying to kill you.

Farther along, half-hidden behind collapsed stone, a narrow side entrance leads into darkness.
story.castle_main_gate_mask:
You step through the broken gate wearing the silver mask.

The courtyard stills.

One ritualist turns. Then another. Then the third lowers his head.

The orcs follow a beat later, weapons dipping toward the stones.

"Master?" one of them asks, voice rough with fear. "Orders?"

The mask whispers:

"Yes. This is how it should be. They should bow to you. You can own all of this. Wouldn't it be nice if you were king?"
story.castle_main_gate_mask_success:
You order them aside.

The ritualists hesitate only long enough to hate obeying you. Then they bow and gesture the orcs back.

The courtyard parts around you, a path opening through blades, tusks, masks, and smoke.
story.castle_main_gate_no_mask:
You step through the broken gate without the silver mask.

The ritualists see you immediately. One points.

The courtyard turns as a single living thing.
story.castle_courtyard_win:
Somehow, impossibly, the courtyard falls quiet around you.

You are alive, but every breath scrapes. This was not the easy route. This was the kind of victory that charges interest.
story.castle_main_hall:
You pass through the inner arch into a main hall lined with cracked statues and old blood.

From here, a servant passage branches toward the quieter parts of the castle.
story.castle_side_entrance:
You find a narrow side passage half-hidden behind collapsed stone. No drums. No chanting. No patrols close enough to hear your breathing.

For the first time since the bridge, you have a moment.

Your health is restored.

Checkpoint saved.
story.castle_kitchen:
The passage opens into a ruined kitchen. Most of the food has spoiled, but you find enough clean cloth, water, and usable supplies to make the search worthwhile.

You pack what you can use.
story.castle_storage: Behind cracked crates and old sacks, you find a small purse holding 30 gold pieces and two sealed health potions.
story.castle_servant_quarters:
The servant quarters are narrow, low-ceilinged, and colder than the rest of the castle.

Most doors hang open. Some rooms are empty. Some are not. Old dwarven bodies lie where they fell, tucked into corners as if the castle got tired of looking at them.
story.servant_room_empty:
The room is empty except for a collapsed bedframe, a cracked washbasin, and finger marks in the dust along the wall.

Someone hid here for a while.

Not long enough.
story.servant_room_bodies:
Three dwarven servants lie beneath a moth-eaten blanket.

Their bones are small under the cloth. One still clutches a brass button from a royal uniform.

No blade marks. No signs of feeding. Just fear arranged into silence.
story.servant_room_ambush:
The barred door gives after one hard shove.

Something inside snarls.

A starving orc and two goblins spill out of the dark, half-mad from being trapped where even the other monsters would not go.
story.servant_room_ambush_win: The last ambusher drops among broken bedposts and old servant shoes.
story.servant_room_ambush_cleared: The barred room is quiet now. Nothing moves inside except dust.
story.servant_room_portrait:
A torn portrait hangs crooked on the wall. Most of the king's face has been burned away, but the little girl beside him remains clear.

Black hair. White face. Small hands wrapped around a familiar doll.
story.servant_note:
The note is brittle enough to crumble at the edges.

"The princess would not sleep without her little doll. If it goes missing again, check the throne room first. She keeps saying it wants to see the king."
story.castle_inner_corridor:
The servant hall narrows into an inner corridor.

The air changes here. Colder. Older. The sounds of the camp fade behind the walls until your own breathing feels like an intrusion.

Ahead, a pair of enormous doors wait beneath a carved crown. To one side, a smaller door hangs half-open, its brass plate scratched but readable: KING'S STUDY.
story.throne_room_pull_known:
Something shifts inside your sack.

You know what it is before you touch it.

The doll.

You pull it free, and its stitched face turns toward the corridor ahead. Not by much. Just enough.

It is pulling you somewhere.
story.throne_room_pull_hidden:
Something tugs at your sack.

At first, you ignore it.

Then the sack pulls hard enough to twist against your shoulder.

You open it and go still.

The doll is inside.

You left it behind. You know you left it behind.

And yet it waits there, black hair tangled around its pale little face, pulling toward the corridor ahead.
story.throne_room_pull_no_doll:
Something cold moves through the corridor ahead, searching for a shape it cannot find.

For one awful second, you feel as if something almost remembers you.
story.throne_room_mask_whisper_stored:
The mask whispers from the sack:

"Let me help you. I can grant you power. You will need it for what's to come."
story.throne_room_mask_whisper_worn:
The mask whispers:

"Yes. We need this. This will let us succeed. We can rule them all with this."
story.throne_room:
At the end of the grand hall stood another pair of sealed doors.

Silver lines had been smeared across the stone like wounds. Eleven circles surrounded one larger circle. Each smaller circle had cracked. The larger circle remained intact, but only just.

The doll began to shake violently.

A whisper pressed itself into your thoughts. Not words. A need. A hunger. A command.

You placed your hand against the stone, and the seals flared. Blue light crawled up your arm. Then the doll jerked in your pack.

The light went black.

The doors opened.

The throne room was vast enough to hold a village. Pillars of black stone rose into darkness above you. Tattered banners hung from the cavern ceiling. The cracked marble floor was veined with silver and stained with something that had seeped too deeply to ever be washed away.

At the far end of the room sat the throne.

And on the throne sat the king.

What remained of him, at least. Carnathis had not rotted like the others. His body was withered, gray, and dry, but intact. His beard lay across his chest like a coil of wire. His fingers still gripped the arms of the throne.

On his head rested the crown.

A simple circlet of dark metal, set with one black stone at its center.

The longer you looked at it, the more certain you became that it was looking back.
story.throne_room_crown_temptation:
The mask whispers:

"The crown can be yours. You just have to give in. Let yourself be a king. Claim the power."
story.throne_room_mask_temptation:
The mask grew heavy in your pack.

A thought slid into your mind, soft and reasonable.

You have carried it this far.

Another thought followed.

You have seen what others could not.

Then another.

The crown belongs to the one strong enough to take it.

Your fingers drifted toward the mask.

For a moment, the throne room changed. The broken pillars stood whole. The banners flew proudly. The throne was no longer a tomb, but a seat of judgment. Armies knelt before you. Dwarves, orcs, ritualists, kings, and priests all bowed their heads.

The crown waited.

The mask waited.

The doll waited.
story.throne_room_resist_mask:
You clenched your fist and pulled your hand away from the mask.

The vision shattered.

The throne room returned: dead banners, broken stone, the corpse on the throne, the crown waiting in silence.
story.mask_corruption_release:
The mask touched your face.

Cold sank into your skin. Then heat. Then pain so sharp it stole the air from your lungs.

You tried to pull the mask away, but your hands stopped halfway to your face. The mask was no longer resting on you.

It was holding you.

Thin roots of shadow slipped beneath your skin, threading through thought, memory, fear, desire.

You remembered your name.

Then you remembered it less.

The dead king lifted his head.

No. Not the dead king. The old shell. The failed vessel.

The crown rose from Carnathis's brow and floated above the throne. The doll flew from your grasp and landed in the corpse's lap. Its stitched mouth split open wider than cloth should allow.

Black smoke poured out. It climbed the walls. It spread across the cavern ceiling. It gathered above the throne in a twisting column of shadow and teeth and reaching limbs.

The creature that emerged was not the weak echo you had seen before.

This was no fragment.

This was an Obliviarch.

The crown descended.

You did not flinch when it settled upon your head.

The throne room shook. Somewhere deep in the castle, the last of the seals broke.

The doors beyond the throne opened.

You walked forward.

The Obliviarch followed.
story.mask_corruption_courtyard:
Beyond the throne room lay the royal courtyard of Carnathis: a vast underground mustering ground carved beneath the mountain. Pillars wider than houses rose into a ceiling lost in darkness. Ancient balconies circled the chamber in broken tiers. Sealed tunnels, watch posts, and old military gates looked down over the stone floor like empty eyes.

This was where the kings beneath the mountain had once gathered their armies.

Now it belonged to invaders.

Ritualists crowded the mustering ground in black robes. Orc warriors stood among them in ranks, their weapons lowered, their armor marked with ash and old blood.

For one breath, every ritualist and every orc stared.

Then the first ritualist dropped to his knees.

The others followed.

A chant rose through the buried courtyard.

"All hail the dark king."

"All hail King Carnathis."

"All hail the eternal king."

A wrongness stirred inside you. Small. Frightened. Human.

This was not your name.

This was not your throne.

This was not your kingdom.

The crown tightened. The mask pulsed.

The wrongness vanished.

Of course they bowed.

Then the Obliviarch moved.

Black tendrils burst from its shadow and shot across the stone floor. They speared into kneeling ritualists, wrapped around orc warriors, climbed pillars, poured through open gates, and vanished into the tunnels beyond.

The front lines screamed. The camp screamed. The chambers you had already passed through screamed.

Every remaining invader beneath the mountain became fuel.

When the last tendrils withdrew, nothing living remained.

Nothing except you.

And the Obliviarch.
story.mask_corruption_order:
Time slowed.

The drifting ash froze in the air. The last falling weapon stopped before it struck the stone. The Obliviarch became still behind you, vast and silent beneath the mountain.

Twelve figures stood at the far end of the underground courtyard.

Eleven remained behind.

One walked forward.

His expression held no awe, no fear, no anger.

Only disgust.

You raised your hand toward him.

"You dare to challenge me? I will destroy you!"

The magic answered.

Then stopped.

Not weakened. Not resisted. Stopped.

As if the world itself had refused to obey.

The man continued walking.

"You are wrong," he said. "And you do not belong here."

The eleven behind him spread out. Pale light unfolded through the courtyard. They did not cleanse an army. The army was already dead. They cleansed what remained of it.

The leader raised one hand toward the Obliviarch.

The creature opened its mouth.

No sound came out.

The leader pointed.

The Obliviarch turned to ash.

No battle. No scream. No resistance.

Then he took the crown from your head and crushed it to dust. He took the mask from your face and broke it in his hand.

Your mind cleared.

You remembered your name.

You were {playerName}.

Not Carnathis. Not king. Not eternal.

Just you.

Alone in the dead courtyard beneath the mountain.

The leader studied you.

"You had potential," he said. "You could have fought, but you are weak."

He stepped closer.

"Do not fret, I will cleanse you."

Warmth flowed from his palm into your body.

For one brief second, it felt like mercy.

Then you understood.

Your fingers turned gray. Your skin broke apart into glowing ash. The last thing you saw was the empty underground courtyard and the man from the Order watching you disappear without pity.
story.doll_summoning:
The doll shrieked.

Not aloud.

Inside your skull.

It tore itself from your grasp and flew across the room. You lunged for it, but it moved too fast, sailing through the stale air before landing in the dead king's lap.

Carnathis's fingers twitched.

The crown rose from his brow.

The doll rose with it.

Both hung above the throne, turning slowly in the air. The black stone in the crown opened like an eye.

Smoke poured out. It did not drift upward. It spilled downward, thick and heavy, rolling across the steps of the throne like ink.

Then something reached through.

A hand, long and black and jointed in too many places, gripped the edge of the smoke.

A second hand followed.

The creature dragged itself into the throne room.

This was the shape the old drawings had tried and failed to warn you about.

An Obliviarch.

Its head turned toward you.

The doll's stitched mouth moved from within its chest.

The creature spoke with a hundred buried voices.

"Vessel refused."

The crown spun faster.

"Then vessel will be broken."
story.obliviarch_phase_one_win:
The Obliviarch staggered, but the throne room itself still worked against you. Cracks spread across the marble. The crown pulsed above the creature. The doll laughed from inside its chest.

Then the old wards answered, faint and blue-white beneath soot, blood, and shadow.
story.magistone_orb_trigger:
The Obliviarch struck faster than you could defend.

A limb of shadow slammed into your chest and hurled you across the throne room. You hit the floor hard. Your weapon skidded away across the cracked marble.

Above you, the Obliviarch unfolded itself to its full height.

The doll in its chest laughed.

Then something in your pack answered.

A pulse of warmth.

The magistone orb rolled free, dull and pale at first, then brightening with every turn. It came to rest beside your hand.

The creature hissed.

Not in anger.

In fear.

You reached for the orb.

The moment your fingers closed around it, heat surged through your body. Living heat. Like sunlight remembered by stone that had never seen the sky.

Then it burst.

White light filled the throne room.

The Obliviarch screamed.

Hidden runes blazed to life across the walls: old dwarven protections buried beneath soot, blood, and shadow. Enough remained.

The warmth rushed through you, knitting flesh, steadying bone, clearing the fog from your mind.

When the light faded, you were standing again.

The magistone orb sat in your palm, but its glow was gone. What had once been a thing of power was now only a smooth stone sphere.

The Obliviarch slowly rose.

It was wounded.

Not defeated.
story.obliviarch_phase_two_intro:
The wards now burned along the walls, weak but awake. The Obliviarch could no longer draw freely from the castle.

The crown's pulse faltered.

The doll in the creature's chest twitched.

For the first time, the monster looked less like doom and more like something that could bleed.
story.obliviarch_phase_two_win:
The Obliviarch staggered.

The wards along the walls burned brighter, their blue-white light cutting through the smoke pouring from its body. The creature reached for the crown, but the crown spun wildly above it, no longer steady, no longer in command.

The doll hung in the hollow of the creature's chest.

For the first time, it looked small.
story.obliviarch_final_strike_prompt:
The warding light pins the Obliviarch in place for one breath.

The crown reels above it.

The doll hangs exposed in its chest.

This is the last opening you are going to get.
story.obliviarch_final_wrong_target:
You strike, but the Obliviarch twists around the blow. The wards flare again, forcing the creature open.

The doll is still the heart of it.
story.obliviarch_destroyed:
You drove your weapon into the doll.

The doll split open.

A sound tore through the throne room, part scream, part sigh, part the last breath of something that had been pretending to live for far too long.

The Obliviarch froze.

Cracks of white light spread across its body.

The crown dropped from the air and struck the floor with a sound like a bell.

Carnathis's corpse slumped on the throne.

The doll burned.

The Obliviarch collapsed inward, folding into itself again and again until nothing remained but a pillar of ash.

The crown blackened. The doll's stitched face curled, shriveled, and vanished.

Then crown, doll, and monster all became dust.

The throne room went silent.

Not peaceful.

Just empty.
story.order_throne_room_arrival:
The throne room doors opened behind you.

You turned, weapon still raised.

Twelve figures entered.

The Order of Lucius.

They moved through the dead throne room without haste. The eleven behind the leader spread out, examining the broken wards, the dead king, the cracked floor, and the place where the Obliviarch had fallen.

None of them looked surprised.

The leader approached with the same indifference he had shown before.

He stopped beside the ash.

"Huh," he said. "It looks like it is already dead."

He looked around the throne room, his expression tightening with disgust.

"With the wards down, we were finally able to see what was happening in this Lucius-forsaken place."

His gaze settled on you.

"You again."

He studied you for a moment.

"I thought you would have succumbed to that doll."
story.order_destroys_stored_mask:
His eyes shifted toward your pack.

"Or the cursed mask."

The mask felt heavier the moment he noticed it.

You removed it slowly.

Even now, after everything, part of you did not want to let it go.

The leader held out one hand.

You gave him the mask.

He looked at it with open contempt.

"Filth."

Pale fire leaked between his fingers. The mask twitched once, almost like something alive.

Then it turned to ash.
story.order_no_mask_comment:
He looked back at you.

"Well," he said, "maybe you do have potential after all."

A pause.

"I will be keeping my eye out for you."

It did not sound like praise.

It sounded like a threat he had not yet decided to carry out.
story.order_cleanses_throne_room:
The eleven completed their work in silence. One traced a symbol of Lucius over the broken throne. Another scattered pale dust over the ash of the Obliviarch. A third pressed a hand to the wall, and the last remaining shadow in the wards burned away.

There were no enemies left to cleanse.

No ritualists.

No orcs.

No corrupted dwarves.

Only residue.

Only memory.

Only the stain of what had happened here.

When they were finished, the leader walked toward the doors.

He did not look back.
story.surviving_route_closing:
You were left alone beneath the mountain.

No wind moved through the halls.

No sunlight touched the stone.

No distant road waited beyond the castle gates.

There was only the vast abandoned kingdom underground: empty halls, dead forges, silent markets, collapsed tunnels, sealed homes, and the throne room of a king who had refused to die.

The Order had cleansed everything else.

The doll was gone.

The crown was gone.

The Obliviarch was gone.

The mask, if you had carried it, was gone too.

For the first time since entering this place, nothing whispered.

That should have comforted you.

It did not.

Without the crown and the doll feeding the old magic, the broken wards began to fail completely. The walls groaned. Dust fell from the ceiling. Somewhere deep below, stone cracked under its own weight.

The mountain was settling.

The kingdom was becoming a tomb again.

You left through the servant passages, moving past abandoned sleeping cells, cold kitchens, broken statues, and bones that no longer had curses clinging to them. Everything was still. Everything was empty.

At the servant entrance, you looked back one last time.

Far behind you, in the direction of the throne room, a final blue-white flash passed through the stone.

Then darkness swallowed it.

The underground kingdom did not collapse.

Not yet.

But it felt as though something ancient had finally exhaled.

You walked on through the buried roads beneath the mountain, carrying no crown, no doll, and no certainty that the Order had spared you out of mercy.

They had cleansed every other being in this place.

Only you remained.

For now.

Suddenly, you hear a voice.

"E-e-excuse me, adventurer?"
story.wazetax_portal_discovery:
"Excuse me, adventurer?"

You turn.

Wazetax stands a few steps behind you, wringing his hands hard enough that you worry one of them might come loose.

"I know this is maybe not a good time," he says, which is impressive because there has not been a good time for several hours. "But while I was hiding, I found something peculiar about the wall near me."

He points back toward the side of the castle.

"There was a seam. Very small. And a bit of old gearwork behind one of the stones. I tinkered with it a little. Just a little."

He swallows.

"It opened. There is a secret entrance. I was too scared to go farther by myself."

After everything you have survived, the sensible part of you suggests leaving immediately.

The adventurer part of you is, unfortunately, still listening.

"Show me," you say.

Wazetax nods and leads you into the dark.
story.wazetax_portal_arch:
Wazetax brings you to a narrow opening in the wall, half-hidden behind fallen stone and shadow.

You squeeze through after him.

The tunnel beyond has no light at all.

No lanterns. No crystals. No old dwarven glow lingering in the stone.

Only darkness, close air, and the scrape of your boots as Wazetax feels his way along the wall ahead.

At last, the passage opens into a small chamber.

It is empty except for an arch.

The arch is freestanding, carved from pale stone and threaded with dark metal. It has no door, no curtain, no stairs beyond it. Just an empty curve of stone waiting in the black.

You stare at it.

"I do not know what this is."

Wazetax makes a sound halfway between a gasp and a squeak.

"That is a teleportation arch."

He steps closer, fear briefly forgotten.

"I studied these in school. Well, drawings of them. Theoretical drawings. Nobody thought any still existed."

He circles the arch, muttering under his breath.

"Anchor stone. Dormant conduit. Old alignment ring. This should not still be intact. Unless... maybe if I plugged this into that..."

A tiny click echoes through the chamber.

All at once, the arch comes to life.

Blue-white light races around the frame. Dust lifts from the floor. The air inside the arch bends, ripples, and opens into a surface like moonlit water.

Wazetax freezes.

The portal wavers, then steadies.

"It still works," he whispers. "But there is no way of knowing what is on the other side. Or if it is even possible to come back."

He looks at the portal for a long moment.

Then he looks at you.

"I am curious," he says, sounding terrified by his own courage. "I am going through."

Before you can stop him, Wazetax steps through the portal and vanishes.

The light shivers once.

Then it steadies again.
story.veyrindel_portal_refusal:
You stand before the portal for a long time.

Long enough for the light to stop feeling like an invitation and start feeling like a warning.

Wazetax is gone.

Maybe he found another world.

Maybe he found another disaster.

Maybe he found exactly the kind of mystery that keeps adventurers walking when every reasonable person would turn around.

Not this time.

You leave the arch behind.

You make your way back through the dark tunnel, out through the secret entrance, and finally out of the cave beneath the dying mountain.

The air outside feels impossibly wide.

You keep walking until Visteria is behind you.

Then you keep walking farther.

In time, people ask where you came from. You tell them less than the truth. In time, your weapon hangs above a quiet hearth instead of at your side.

You retire from the adventurer life.

Some doors are meant to stay closed.

Some portals are meant for someone else.

Your adventure ends here.
story.veyrindel_portal_score:
You step toward the portal.

Whatever waits beyond it is unknown.

Whatever path leads back, if one exists, is hidden from you.

But Wazetax went through.

And after everything Visteria has taken, erased, buried, and broken, you are not ready to let the story end at the edge of a doorway.

Portal to Veyrindel discovered.

Score modifier unlocked: +500.
story.veyrindel_portal_placeholder:
You step through the portal.

Light closes around you.

For one breath, there is no mountain, no castle, no Visteria.

Only motion.

Only distance.

Only the impossible feeling of a world opening somewhere ahead.

The path to Veyrindel will be added later.
story.false_hydra_final_win:
The last red eye gutters out.

The Obliviarch collapses into ash, shadow, and the terrible relief of remembering what it tried to erase.

The doll lies still in the blackened bloom. The crown remains on the king's skull, cracked from rim to point.

For now, the throne room is only a room again.
story.bridge_prepare_success: You bind wounds and steady your hands.
story.bridge_sneak_start:
You step onto the bridge and move with the smoke.

Boots scrape against stone somewhere ahead. Goblins mutter near a lantern. An orc turns just as you slip behind a cracked slab of bridgework.
story.bridge_sneak_success: You pass between patrols, ducking from shadow to shadow until the bridge widens around a warehouse built into the structure itself.
story.bridge_sneak_fail: A goblin spots movement and shrieks. The nearest patrol turns toward you with weapons raised.
story.bridge_patrol_defeated: The patrol falls. Their bodies vanish into the smoke as the bridge rumbles beneath your feet.
story.bridge_run_to_traps: You escape the patrol, but not cleanly. Orc horns bark behind you, boots hammer across stone, and the only open path is forward onto the trapped dwarven road.
story.bridge_orc_fight_start:
You give up on subtlety and step into the open.

Four orcs notice immediately. Weapons rise. Boots pound against the bridge stone. If the bridge wanted quiet, it should not have invited you.
story.bridge_orc_fight_defeated: The fourth orc hits the stone hard and does not get back up. The smoke swallows the bodies while the path ahead opens toward the warehouse built into the bridge.
story.warehouse_enter:
About a quarter of the way across, you find a warehouse built into the bridge structure.

Inside, a ritual is already in progress. A man wearing a silver mask stands at the center, leading the chant. Four cultists in robes and bronze masks guard the ritual circle.

Every instinct in your body screams the same thing: this ritual must be stopped at all costs.
story.warehouse_run_caught:
You break for the warehouse exit, but the cultists were waiting for that.

A bronze-masked figure slams into your shoulder. Hands seize your arms and drag you back across the stone toward the ritual circle.

The chanting never stops.

You can still try to fight your way free, or you can sit there and watch what happens.
story.warehouse_fight_back: You wrench one arm free and throw yourself back into the fight.
story.warehouse_watch_ritual:
You stop fighting.

The cultists force you to your knees at the edge of the circle while the silver-masked leader raises his hands.

The ritual answers.

Violet light opens like an eye, and whatever waits beyond it notices you first.
story.warehouse_cultists_defeated: The last bronze-masked cultist drops, and the ritual circle sputters like a dying flame.
story.warehouse_leader_rage:
The silver-masked leader turns toward you.

"You! You have it. I need it. Give it to me!"

His voice tears into a snarl. The mask flashes, and he flies into a rage.
story.warehouse_leader_rage_known_doll:
The silver-masked leader turns toward you.

His attention snaps to your sack, to the shape of the doll hidden there.

"You! You have it. Give it to me!"

His voice tears into a snarl. The mask flashes, and he flies into a rage.
story.warehouse_leader_rage_hidden_doll:
The silver-masked leader's head turns sharply toward your sack, as if he can see through cloth and leather.

"You have it," he snarls. "Even if you do not know it yet. Give it to me."

The mask flashes, and he flies into a rage.
story.warehouse_leader_rage_no_doll:
The silver-masked leader turns toward you and stops.

"No," he whispers. "Then why did the ritual pull you here?"

His confusion lasts only a moment. Then the mask flashes, and he flies into a rage.
story.warehouse_leader_defeated: The masked leader collapses beside the broken ritual circle. For a moment, everything is quiet except the lava groaning beneath the bridge.
story.silver_mask_choice:
The silver mask does not fall like dead metal.

It rests in the ash, untouched by blood or heat, and your eyes keep returning to it. Power radiates from its surface in slow, patient waves. It feels ancient. It feels dangerous.

It also feels like it is waiting for you.
story.silver_mask_leave_confirm:
You turn away, but the pull remains behind your eyes.

Are you absolutely sure you want to leave the silver mask behind?
story.silver_mask_left: You leave the silver mask in the ash and force yourself toward the far side of the warehouse.
story.silver_mask_stored: You wrap the silver mask carefully and store it away. Even hidden from sight, it feels heavier than it should.
story.silver_mask_worn:
You lift the silver mask and press it to your face.

Cold power floods your body. Your wounds tighten. Your grip strengthens. The world sharpens until every breath of smoke, every spark of lava, every distant footstep feels painfully clear.

Health +6. Damage +3.

For now, the mask is silent.
story.warehouse_ritual_surges:
The silver mask grows cold in your hands.

For a brief moment, silence fills the warehouse.

Then the ritual circle pulses.

Once.

Twice.

The blood markings spread across the floor like living veins as the air itself begins to vibrate. The remaining candles flare violently, their flames twisting into unnatural shapes.

The ritual is still active.

And it is growing stronger.

A low sound begins to echo from the center of the circle. Not a voice. Not quite. More like reality itself groaning under pressure.

The center of the ritual slowly tears open.

At first it is no larger than a coin, suspended in the air like a crack in glass. Then it widens. Darkness spills from within, deeper than shadow, swallowing the light around it.

Heat pours from the opening.

Something moves inside.

Your instincts scream at you to act.

What do you do?
story.warehouse_leave:
You turn and sprint for the exit as the warehouse trembles behind you.

The magistone lanterns outside begin to flicker violently as a deafening roar erupts from within the structure. The bridge itself groans beneath your feet.

You risk a glance backward.

The hole has expanded to consume nearly the entire ritual chamber.

Then something emerges.

A massive ashen-gray head forces itself through the opening, its maw splitting unnaturally wide with rows of jagged teeth grinding against one another.

Then another head appears.

And another.

Seven grotesque heads writhe and snap as they drag themselves from the darkness beyond. Their malformed body twists unnaturally, supported by massive pincers and disturbingly human-like hands that claw against the stone floor.

The creature pulls itself fully into the world.
story.warehouse_mask_whisper_stayed:
And somewhere deep inside your mind, you hear the mask whisper:

"You should have stayed."
story.false_hydra_interruption:
The creature's heads open in a sound that is almost a song and almost an argument reality is losing.
