The warehouse collapses around it as the false hydra drags itself fully into the world. Pale throats stretch through the smoke. Too many mouths open at once. Every memory of the room starts trying to invent reasons not to notice it.

Every instinct in your body screams the same thing.

Run.

Or fight.

No matter your choice...

Everything stops.

The creature freezes mid-motion.

The falling ash hangs motionless in the air.

Even the lava below the bridge stills into perfect silence.

The world itself feels as though it is holding its breath.

Then you feel it.

A presence.

Cold.

Ancient.

Watching.
story.order_mask_warning:
The silver mask clamps cold against your face.

A whisper cuts through the frozen silence.

"Hide me. Now. Do not let them see."

Your hands claw at the edge of the mask.

It does not move.

So you bow your head, turn away from the Order's gaze, and feel the thing on your face smile without lips.
story.mask_corruption_deepens: The silver mask settles deeper. Something inside it remembers that you chose this. Corruption: {corruption}.
story.order_arrival:
Slow footsteps echo through the warehouse entrance.

One by one, figures emerge from the shadows.

Tall.

Armored.

Each wears gleaming white mithril-like armor draped in deep purple cloaks. Their faces are hidden behind flawless white masks devoid of expression.

There are twelve of them.

The figure leading them stands taller than the others, his cloak dragging across the broken stone floor as he approaches the frozen creature without even acknowledging its presence.

Then his masked face turns toward you.

"It appears," he says calmly, "that you have discovered something particularly nasty."

You cannot tell whether he means the monster...

Or something else.
story.order_dialogue_who:
The figure folds his hands behind his back.

"You stand before the Order of Lucius."

His tone carries the same weight as someone announcing the sunrise.

"We are the hand that restores order when lesser beings fail to do so."

One of the masked warriors behind him lowers their head slightly in reverence at the mention of the name.

"Where chaos festers, we purge it. Where corruption spreads, we burn it away."

Though his words sound noble...

The way he speaks makes it painfully clear that he views you as insignificant.
story.order_dialogue_monster:
The masked figure slowly turns toward the frozen abomination.

"A false hydra."

Even saying the name makes your thoughts slide away from the creature's outline.

"A predator of memory. It feeds, and the world politely explains away the empty places it leaves behind."

One of the creature's heads twitches slightly despite time itself appearing frozen.

The man does not react.

"It should not exist here."

For the first time, his voice carries the faintest trace of irritation.
story.order_dialogue_why:
"Because this world requires order."

The response comes immediately.

"This creature threatens the balance established by Lucius."

He slowly walks past you toward the frozen monster.

"And so we will remove it."

There is no compassion in his voice.

No fear.

No hesitation.

Only certainty.

The figure pauses briefly before continuing.

"The will of Lucius is not questioned. It is obeyed."

Something about the way he says it sends a chill through your entire body.
story.order_departure:
The masked figure studies you silently for a moment.

Then he speaks.

"You would do well to leave this place."

His tone is calm, yet absolute. Not a suggestion.

A command.

Behind him, the eleven masked warriors spread out across the ruined warehouse with practiced precision while the frozen false hydra remains suspended in place like a grotesque statue.

The figure turns away from you as though the conversation is already over.

"This manifestation belongs to the Order now."

For reasons you cannot fully explain...

You listen.

You back away from the warehouse entrance before finally turning and making your way down the bridge. The heat from the molten lake below presses against your skin while distant echoes of chanting still linger in your mind.

Then suddenly...

*POP*

The sound is small.

Sharp.

Wrong.

You immediately turn around.

The warehouse is gone.

The ritual circle is gone.

The portal is gone.

The false hydra is gone.

Even the damage from the battle has vanished.

Only an empty stretch of bridge remains behind you, illuminated by rows of softly glowing magistone lanterns.

It is as if nothing ever existed there at all.

No blood.

No bodies.

No sign that anyone had ever been present.

A cold feeling settles into your stomach.
story.order_mask_missed_one:
The silver mask beneath your cloak feels warm.

And for just a brief moment...

You swear you hear whispering.

"They missed one."
story.silver_mask_reworn:
Your hands move before you fully decide to move them.

You put the silver mask back on.

The cold weight settles over your face, and a breath you did not know you were holding leaves your chest. Relief rolls through you first. Then power.

The bridge feels quieter with the mask in place.
story.silver_mask_put_on_after_order:
You take out the silver mask and put it on.

The moment it touches your face, the fear in your chest loosens. Strength rushes through your limbs, cold and clean and far too welcome.

Health +6. Damage +3.

You try to lift it away again, just to prove you can.

It does not move.

The choice has teeth now.
story.warehouse_stop_ritual:
You lunge for the ritual circle and drive your weapon through the nearest line of living blood.

The mark splits. The circle screams without a mouth. For one terrible second, you think the opening might collapse.

Then the hole pulls back.

Not closed.

Coiling.

The tear widens faster, chewing through the floorboards and dragging broken stone toward its center. The chant dies, but the ritual does not. It has stopped needing voices.

Whatever is coming through is no longer waiting.
story.warehouse_jump_hole:
You jump into the hole.

For an instant, there is heat, pressure, and the feeling of falling in every direction at once.

Then the world closes behind you.
story.warehouse_fail: The battle breaks you against the warehouse floor.
story.bridge_navigation_start:
The patrols fall behind you, but the bridge narrows into an old dwarven kill road.

Four worn direction markers jut from the stone. The map is gone now, brittle scraps lost to ash and wind.

Choose the first direction.
story.bridge_wrong:
The stone under your foot sinks with a hollow click.
The bridge trap opens beneath you.
story.bridge_success: You follow the final direction and clear the trapped road without waking the old dwarven defenses.
story.production:
The production area is a maze of chains, hooks, furnaces, and dead machines.
One wrong turn will end you.

Choose the first path.
story.production_success:
You find the hidden maintenance lift and ride it toward moonlight.
Chapter complete. You have escaped the buried city of Visteria.
story.production_fail: The floor gives way beneath you. The production area keeps its secrets.
story.complete: You have completed this playable chapter of Tales of Visteria.
story.game_over_footer:
Thanks for playing.
This was made in memory of a DnD session with friends.
game_over.hydra:
The hydra was not an encounter. It was a weather event with teeth.
The story ends before anyone can object.
game_over.forest_mind_break:
The forest repeats one time too many.
Your thoughts unravel into gray miasma, and you wander beneath the same trees for the rest of your life.
game_over.ghost_black_pits:
The pale girl's eyes become empty black pits.
You lose your sense of self, then the dark takes the rest.
game_over.combat:
The fight turns against you.
Steel, teeth, and panic close in until Visteria falls silent again.
game_over.warehouse:
The warehouse ritual notices you.
Whatever waits inside that violet light accepts your life as payment.
game_over.ritual_hole: The ritual opening accepts you before you understand what waits beyond it.
game_over.orc_party: The party does not end when you fall. It gets louder. Orc blades, boots, and laughter close over you until Visteria takes the last of your breath.
game_over.ale_explosion: The ale barrel erupts too close. Fire, splinters, and burning drink take you before the orcs even understand what happened.
game_over.castle_courtyard: The courtyard closes around you under ritual masks and orc blades.
game_over.false_hydra:
The false hydra sings your absence into the world.
By the time anyone realizes something is missing, memory has already learned to look away.
game_over.obliviarch:
The Obliviarch corrects the world around your absence.
By the time you realize something is missing, reality has already decided it was you.
game_over.mask_corruption:
The Order of Lucius cleanses the crown, the mask, the Obliviarch, and the false king you became.
For one breath, you remember your name. Then even that turns to ash.
game_over.bridge_trap:
The bridge defenses wake beneath your feet.
Without the right path, the old city claims you as another warning.
game_over.production:
The production tunnels shift, bite, and open beneath you.
The buried city keeps its secrets.
game_over.mimic:
The chest was not treasure. It was hunger pretending to be useful.
Your adventure ends between wooden teeth.
game_over.manor_chest:
The black chest detonates before you can let go of the key.
The buried city keeps one more secret, and you become part of the wreckage.
game_over.default:
You fall in the dark beneath Visteria.
Start a new game to try another route.
story.kings_study:
The king's study has not been looted. That alone feels wrong.

Maps cover one wall, each marked with red pins and frantic notes: breached watchtowers, border villages gone silent, caravans found empty, scouts reporting more orcs and worse things moving under unfamiliar banners. A stack of military reports describes invasion after invasion pressing closer to Visteria. In the margins, the king's hand grows less steady.

They will overrun us.

The army cannot hold every gate.

If the old wards are real, I must wake something stronger than an army.

On the desk, beneath a cracked paperweight, you find a ledger of prisoners, condemned criminals, and volunteers whose names have been crossed out. Beside them are ritual quantities written in a red-brown ink: blood, breath, memory, royal oath. One line is underlined so hard the quill tore the page.

Sacrifices must be made before the kingdom is sacrificed entire.

The research turns from military reports to summoning theory. The king had been searching for ways to call eldritch beasts into the world, not as invaders, but as weapons. One page shows a pale many-headed thing labeled False Hydra. Even as an ink drawing, it makes you shudder. The notes say its song hollows memory, steals the knowledge of the dead, and leaves survivors inventing reasons for the empty chairs around them.

You turn the page.

The next image is black as night, with red glowing eyes. Eleven heads rise from an almost serpentine body braced on clawed feet. You cannot tell whether the shapes along its sides are wings or fins. The picture looks almost alive, as if the ink is deciding which version of itself should be true.

OBLIVIARCH, the caption reads.

The tome describes it as an anti-narrative beast: an aura that causes reality to correct itself around what the creature erases; a howl that can scour a person from the present; a mind that experiences time out of order; a hunger not for flesh, but for evidence that something ever existed.

At the bottom of the page, the king wrote only one sentence:

If invasion is certain, may history forgive what survival requires.
choice.dm_sneak_past_party: Let them sneak past the party
choice.dm_succumb_mask: Let the mask have its moment
story.dm_orc_tents: They duck into the tent line, which is bold because tents are just fabric rooms full of consequences.
story.dm_orc_tents_success: Somehow, they slip through without kicking a bucket, stepping on a hand, or asking a tarp to betray them. Growth.
story.dm_orc_tents_fail:
They kick a shovel. A shovel. The least threatening object in camp becomes the alarm bell.

Fight setup: one offended orc.
story.dm_orc_barrels: They crouch behind the barrels, because apparently storage is cover now. Fine. I respect furniture-based tactics.
story.dm_orc_barrels_success: The barrels do their one job. The orcs never look over. Incredible. Someone give those barrels inspiration.
story.dm_orc_barrels_fail:
An orc grabs the exact barrel they are hiding behind. Betrayed by inventory management.

Fight setup: one orc who wanted a drink and found a problem.
story.dm_orc_fire_fight:
They walk through the middle like subtlety was banned at session zero.

Fight setup: three orcs by the fire, then one more from the tents because tents have consequences.
story.dm_orc_inner_path: Past the outer camp, the path narrows between broken shields and old dwarven beams. Ahead is the party area, which is less an encounter and more a poor labor decision with music.
story.dm_orc_party_area:
Thirty orcs are packed around a fire pit, drinking, shouting, and being every reason I own too many dice.

The cages are beyond them. Naturally.
story.dm_orc_party_sneak: They sneak around the edge of the party while I pretend not to notice that this is smarter than fighting thirty orcs. Annoying. Effective.
story.dm_orc_party_distraction: A distraction, yes. Classic player thinking: if the problem is huge, make a different problem and run while everyone compares disasters.
story.dm_orc_party_ale_hurt: The ale barrel catches too well. Fire, foam, screaming, and a blast of hot stupid roll across the camp.
story.dm_orc_party_ale_success: The ale barrel erupts into glorious workplace negligence. Orcs scatter. Someone yells for water. Someone else yells for more ale. Perfect.
story.dm_orc_party_ale_fail: The barrel smokes, sputters, and refuses to become dramatic. I have never been more disappointed in alcohol.
story.dm_orc_party_rock_fail: They throw a rock. It hits another rock. For one majestic second, the entire camp ignores them. That was the whole plan. Amazing.
story.dm_orc_party_fight:
They start a fight with the party group.

Fight setup: three orcs up front. The other twenty-seven are currently discovering object permanence.
story.dm_orc_party_fight_warning:
The first orcs drop, and then the rest of the party starts turning around.

This is the part where smart players leave. So naturally I need to offer the other button.
story.dm_orc_party_fight_disaster: They keep fighting. Thirty orcs. Six attacking at a time. This is not bravery. This is a math crime with weapons.
story.dm_orc_cages:
Past the party area, the cages stink of blood, rust, and bad decisions. Two prisoners are dead. One is alive, terrified, and trying very hard not to be noticed.

His name is Wazetax, which is exactly the kind of name you get when a tinkerer survives long enough to become plot-relevant.
story.dm_wazetax_rescue:
They open the cage. Wazetax practically folds himself out of it, all elbows, panic, and gratitude.

Great. The party has acquired a nervous side quest with knees.
story.dm_wazetax_left:
They leave Wazetax in the cage.

Cold. Efficient. Morally questionable. The kind of choice players defend by saying, "We were low on resources," as if that helps.
story.dm_castle_front_wazetax: They reach the castle front with Wazetax crouched behind them like a man trying to stealth-roll with disadvantage against his own breathing.
story.dm_wazetax_who: Wazetax explains that he is a tinkerer. Broken hinges, stuck locks, busted gears, old traps. Useful, theoretically. Currently shaped like fear.
story.dm_wazetax_happened: He says orcs grabbed him near the ruins and threw him in the cage. Very concise. Trauma does wonders for editing.
story.dm_wazetax_knows: He does not know the big mystery, which is good, because I worked hard on that. He knows the castle is wrong and everyone with survival instincts avoids it.
story.dm_wazetax_hide: They tell Wazetax to hide. He vanishes around the side of the castle with the energy of a man who has finally been given an achievable objective.
story.dm_mask_corruption:
The mask wins.

The party becomes a crown-shaped problem in a throne room full of old sins. The Order eventually solves the problem in the least comforting way possible.
story.dm_obliviarch_phase_one:
Desktop combat: Obliviarch, phase one.

It hits like a forgotten god having a bad day, which is rude because I was saving that phrase for something later.
story.dm_magistone_orb_trigger:
The magistone orb finally does the thing I absolutely planned the whole time. White light, old wards, dramatic scream, full heal.

See? Foreshadowing. Not hoarding. Foreshadowing.
story.dm_obliviarch_phase_two_intro: The wards wake up along the walls. The Obliviarch is wounded now, which means the fight moves from impossible to extremely rude.
story.dm_obliviarch_phase_two:
Desktop combat: wounded Obliviarch, phase two.

The crown is wobbling, the doll is exposed, and the creature is officially having the worst family reunion in history.
story.dm_final_strike_prompt:
The wards pin the Obliviarch open for one breath. Crown above. Doll in the chest. Monster screaming.

Final target selection. No pressure, except all of it.
story.dm_final_strike_wrong_target:
Wrong target. The wards flare and shove the creature open again.

The doll is still the heart of it. I am not even being subtle anymore.
story.dm_obliviarch_destroyed:
They strike the doll.

The scream is awful, the crown drops, the king finally slumps like a corpse should, and the Obliviarch folds into ash.

That is a boss death. Put a little respect on my staging.
story.dm_order_throne_room_arrival: The Order enters the throne room like they had a calendar invite. Twelve white masks, zero urgency, and exactly the energy of people who know more than they are saying.
story.dm_order_destroys_stored_mask:
They find the stored silver mask and destroy it.

Rude. Correct, probably, but rude.
story.dm_order_no_mask_comment: They find no loose mask problem to confiscate. I can feel the campaign notes quietly closing one dangerous tab.
story.dm_order_cleanses_throne_room: The Order cleanses the room, the ash, the throne, the old wards, and several things I was saving for ominous callbacks. Fine. Very professional. Deeply annoying.
story.dm_surviving_route_closing:
They leave the throne room alive. The kingdom is quiet now, which should feel like victory and mostly feels like waiting for the next awful sentence.

Then Wazetax clears his throat somewhere ahead.

Of course he does.
